items.question_text: Use FIG 3-7-1. An aircraft on a vertically guided final is within 2 miles of the threshold, ceiling is 250 feet, and a vehicle is in the POFZ. What must ATC ensure?
items.explanation: FIG 3-7-1 supports the POFZ clearance rule. When the aircraft is within 2 miles and ceiling is below 300 feet or visibility is less than 3/4 SM, the POFZ must be clear of traffic.

choices.text: Ensure the POFZ is clear of aircraft and vehicles.
Allow the vehicle to remain if the aircraft is not yet over the threshold.
Clear only the runway surface; the POFZ does not matter until touchdown.
Issue takeoff clearance to move traffic through the POFZ quickly.

items.question_text: Use FIG 3-7-1. A vertically guided approach is within 2 miles, weather is below the OCS trigger, and ATC cannot clear the final approach OCS before the aircraft reaches that point. What is the required fallback?
items.explanation: Paragraph 3-7-6, illustrated by FIG 3-7-1, requires traffic to the landing aircraft if the POFZ or OCS cannot be cleared before the 2-mile point in the specified weather.

choices.text: Cancel the approach clearance automatically.
Issue traffic to the landing aircraft, framed for a possible missed approach.
Ignore vehicles less than 20 feet high anywhere on the airport.
Delay traffic information until the pilot initiates a missed approach.

items.question_text: Use FIG 3-9-9. A departure is waiting on one runway while a preceding aircraft departs from an intersecting runway. When may the waiting departure begin takeoff roll?
items.explanation: FIG 3-9-9 illustrates the intersecting-runway departure-behind-departure condition: the preceding aircraft must pass the intersection or turn to avert conflict.

choices.text: When the preceding aircraft is airborne, regardless of position.
When the preceding aircraft has passed the intersection or is turning to avert conflict.
When both aircraft have acknowledged traffic information.
Only after the preceding aircraft crosses the runway end.

items.question_text: Use FIG 3-9-10. A preceding arrival has completed landing roll on an intersecting runway and acknowledged instructions to hold short of the intersection. May a departure on the other runway begin takeoff roll under this condition?
items.explanation: FIG 3-9-10 is tied to the preceding-arrival condition. A completed landing roll plus acknowledged hold-short instruction at the intersection is one of the listed ways to satisfy separation.

choices.text: Yes, that is one listed condition for the preceding-arrival case.
No, the arrival must be physically clear of all runways in every case.
No, hold-short instructions apply only to taxiing aircraft.
Yes, but only if the arrival is Category I.

items.question_text: Use FIG 3-9-12 and FIG 3-9-13. A Category F aircraft will take off behind a Category C aircraft on an intersecting runway and the projected flight paths cross. What wake interval applies?
items.explanation: For intersecting runway flight paths crossing, FIG 3-9-12 and FIG 3-9-13 support a 2-minute interval for Category E, F, G, H, or I aircraft behind Category C.

choices.text: No interval; intersecting-runway rules replace wake intervals.
2 minutes.
3 minutes.
4 minutes.

items.question_text: Use FIG 3-9-11. In the LAHSO intersecting-runway departure case, what must the arriving aircraft have acknowledged before it can be part of the separation solution?
items.explanation: FIG 3-9-11 corresponds to the condition where the arriving aircraft has acknowledged a clearance to land and hold short of the intersecting runway or intersecting flight path.

choices.text: A clearance to land and hold short of the intersecting runway or flight path.
Only traffic information about the departing aircraft.
A missed approach instruction.
A clearance to cross the intersecting runway after landing.

items.question_text: Use FIG 3-9-14. A preceding aircraft has departed from a nonintersecting converging runway and its flight path intersects the departure path. What must occur before the next departure begins takeoff roll?
items.explanation: FIG 3-9-14 illustrates the condition that the preceding aircraft has departed and crossed the departure runway, or is turning to avert conflict.

choices.text: The preceding aircraft must cross the departure runway or turn to avert conflict.
The preceding aircraft only needs to be issued traffic.
The next departure may roll once the preceding aircraft is airborne.
The next departure must wait until radar separation exists.

items.question_text: Use FIG 3-9-15 and FIG 3-9-16. A preceding arrival on a converging runway has completed landing roll and will hold short of the projected intersection. What does this permit for the departure runway?
items.explanation: FIG 3-9-15 and FIG 3-9-16 illustrate that a preceding arrival may satisfy the condition after landing roll if it will hold short of the projected intersection, has passed it, or has crossed over the departure runway.

choices.text: It may satisfy converging-runway separation for the departure.
It never satisfies separation because the runways do not physically intersect.
It satisfies separation only if LAHSO is unavailable.
It satisfies separation only for same-runway departures.

items.question_text: Use FIG 3-9-17 and FIG 3-9-18. If an extended centerline crosses a converging runway or its extended centerline within 1 NM of either departure end, what rule set normally applies?
items.explanation: FIG 3-9-17 and FIG 3-9-18 illustrate the 1 NM trigger. In that geometry, apply 3-9-8 intersecting runway/intersecting flight path operations unless facility-specified aids are used.

choices.text: Same-runway separation under 3-9-6.
Intersecting runway/intersecting flight path operations under 3-9-8, unless approved facility aids apply.
Only wake turbulence advisories; no separation rule is triggered.
Opposite-direction parallel runway minima.

items.question_text: Use FIG 3-9-19 and FIG 3-9-20. A Category I aircraft will take off behind a Category E aircraft on a converging runway and projected flight paths cross. What wake interval applies?
items.explanation: FIG 3-9-19 and FIG 3-9-20 support a 2-minute interval for Category I behind Category E when converging-runway projected flight paths cross.

choices.text: No interval if the runways do not physically intersect.
2 minutes.
3 minutes.
6,000 feet.

items.question_text: Use FIG 3-9-21. An aircraft's taxi route crosses another runway before the departure runway, and the aircraft can completely cross and hold short before takeoff clearance. What must happen before takeoff clearance is issued?
items.explanation: FIG 3-9-21 illustrates runway/taxiway proximity where all runways along the taxi route leading to the departure runway must be crossed before takeoff clearance, unless the narrow-configuration exception applies.

choices.text: The intervening runway must be crossed before issuing takeoff clearance.
The takeoff clearance may include an implied runway crossing.
The aircraft may be cleared for takeoff once it reports ready.
The controller should omit the departure runway number to avoid confusion.

items.question_text: Use FIG 3-9-22. Airport geometry prevents an aircraft from fully crossing an intervening runway and holding short of the departure runway. How should the takeoff clearance handle the crossing?
items.explanation: FIG 3-9-22 supports the exception: state the runway to be crossed with the takeoff clearance when the aircraft cannot complete the crossing before reaching the departure runway.

choices.text: Do not mention the crossed runway; the takeoff clearance implies it.
State the runway to be crossed with the takeoff clearance.
Use only taxi phraseology and issue takeoff clearance later.
Use 'full length' to clarify the departure runway.

items.question_text: Use FIG 3-10-2. Between sunrise and sunset, landmarks show 3,000 feet from the landing threshold. May a Category I arrival cross the threshold behind a landed Category I aircraft that is not yet clear?
items.explanation: FIG 3-10-2 supports the daylight landmark-distance alternative: Category I landing behind Category I or II requires 3,000 feet from the landing threshold.

choices.text: Yes, if the landmark-distance condition is met.
No, a landed aircraft must always be clear of the runway.
No, Category I behind Category I requires 4,500 feet.
Yes, but only if wake turbulence separation is waived.

items.question_text: Use FIG 3-10-3. Between sunrise and sunset, a Category II aircraft is landing behind a landed Category II aircraft that is not yet clear. What landmark distance from the threshold is required?
items.explanation: FIG 3-10-3 supports the Category II arrival behind Category I or II daylight alternative: 4,500 feet from the landing threshold.

choices.text: 3,000 feet.
4,500 feet.
6,000 feet.
2 minutes.

items.question_text: Use FIG 3-10-5. An arriving aircraft follows a departing aircraft on the same runway, and one of the aircraft is Category III. The departure is airborne but has not crossed the runway end. What landmark distance from the landing threshold is required?
items.explanation: FIG 3-10-5 illustrates the same-runway arrival behind departure alternative: when either aircraft is Category III, 6,000 feet from the landing threshold is required.

choices.text: 3,000 feet.
4,500 feet.
6,000 feet.
No distance minimum once the departure is airborne.

items.question_text: Use FIG 3-10-4. An arriving aircraft follows a departure from the same runway. What is the basic non-landmark condition before the arrival crosses the landing threshold?
items.explanation: FIG 3-10-4 corresponds to the basic same-runway arrival-behind-departure condition: the other aircraft has departed and crossed the runway end.

choices.text: The departure has crossed the runway end.
The departure is aligned with runway heading.
The departure has been issued traffic.
The departure has reached 400 feet AGL.

items.question_text: Use FIG 3-11-6. Two helicopters will conduct simultaneous takeoffs from separate points. What minimum distance between takeoff points is required if the courses do not conflict?
items.explanation: FIG 3-11-6 supports the simultaneous helicopter/powered-lift rule: at least 200 feet between landing or takeoff points and nonconflicting courses.

choices.text: 100 feet.
200 feet.
300 feet.
700 feet.

items.question_text: Use FIG 3-12-2. On the same sea lane, a departing seaplane follows an airborne Category II aircraft. Landmarks show 3,000 feet between aircraft. Is that enough under the landmark-distance alternative?
items.explanation: FIG 3-12-2 supports 3,000 feet when a Category I aircraft is preceded by Category II or when either the succeeding or both are Category II. Category III would require 6,000 feet.

choices.text: Yes, 3,000 feet satisfies the Category II sea-lane departure alternative.
No, all sea-lane departures require 6,000 feet.
No, same sea-lane departures use only time intervals.
Yes, but only if both aircraft are Category III.

items.question_text: Use FIG 3-12-1. For same sea-lane departure separation without using the landmark-distance alternative, what must the preceding departure do?
items.explanation: FIG 3-12-1 corresponds to the basic same-sea-lane departure condition: the other aircraft has departed and crossed the end of the sea lane or turned to avert conflict.

choices.text: Cross the end of the sea lane or turn to avert conflict.
Report clear of wake turbulence.
Reach 1,500 feet above the water.
Maintain runway heading until advised.

items.question_text: Use FIG 3-12-3. A Category I aircraft is landing behind a landed Category I aircraft on the same sea lane during daylight. Landmarks show 2,000 feet from the landing threshold. May the arrival cross the threshold before the other aircraft has taxied out?
items.explanation: FIG 3-12-3 supports 2,000 feet for Category I landing behind Category I or II on the same sea lane under the daylight landmark-distance alternative.

choices.text: Yes, if the 2,000-foot landmark-distance condition is met.
No, the preceding aircraft must always taxi out first.
No, Category I requires 2,500 feet.
Yes, but only behind a departing aircraft.

items.question_text: Use FIG 3-12-4. A Category II aircraft is landing behind a landed Category II aircraft on the same sea lane during daylight. What landmark distance from the landing threshold is required?
items.explanation: FIG 3-12-4 supports 2,500 feet when a Category II aircraft is landing behind Category I or II on the same sea lane.

choices.text: 1,500 feet.
2,000 feet.
2,500 feet.
6,000 feet.

items.question_text: Use FIG 3-12-6. An arrival follows a departing aircraft on the same sea lane; one aircraft is Category III and the departure is airborne but has not crossed the end. What landmark distance from the landing threshold is required?
items.explanation: FIG 3-12-6 supports 6,000 feet when either aircraft is Category III for same sea-lane arrival behind departure.

choices.text: 1,500 feet.
3,000 feet.
6,000 feet.
No distance minimum once airborne.
